ollama_format.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
ollama_format.title: StructuredSmokeText

structured_output_schema.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
structured_output_schema.title: StructuredSmokeText

parsed_response.response_text: The number of households engaged in social learning (household-SL? = true) follows a variable but structured trend across timesteps and simulations. Initially, no households exhibit social learning behavior in the first five timesteps (columns 0–4), consistent with baseline environmental conditions and household starting behaviors. From timestep 5 onward, counts emerge unevenly across runs, peaking gradually in later timesteps (e.g., 15–20+ households in some instances by timesteps 20–25), before stabilizing or declining slightly in later years. The maximum observed count reaches 54 households (column 29, row 40), suggesting rare instances of near-complete population engagement. Variability across simulations reflects stochastic environmental stresses (e.g., rainfall variability via global-rain? = True) and agent-level behavioral strategies, with slower adoption in lowered-risk scenarios and faster adoption in harsher conditions. While no systematic directional trend dominates, the data suggests social learning adoption is contingent on intermediate-to-late-stage systemic pressures, with delayed activation aligning with cumulative learning-by-experience or environmental feedback accumulation. No clear saturation pattern emerges by the final timestep, hinting at residual variability in household strategy adaptation.

The simulation runs for 29 timesteps (years), and each column represents the count of households using social learning (household-SL?) at each timestep.

- Around timestep 5 onwards, counts start to emerge in more runs, slowly increasing over time.

The trend is an initial latency (first 5 years no SL), followed by gradual or abrupt emergence, peaking after several years before stabilizing or declining. This suggests that under certain conditions, even with low social learning propensity, environmental stresses or other factors might trigger households to engage in SL.
